food_subgroup: Marinated grilled chicken with rice and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken bulgogi dosirak; FOODON_00002764 (chicken dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken bulgogi dosirak; A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as closest defensible parent. The dish also contains rice and side dishes not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chicken bulgogi dosirak or sufficiently close Korean lunchbox dish.

main_ingredients: bulgogi marinade (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, gochujang or gochugaru)
banchan side dishes (typically kimchi, seasoned vegetables)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dosirak (lunchbox) serving of approximately 500 g, comprising marinated chicken bulgogi, steamed white rice, two pork and chive mandu, and two banchan side dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken bulgogi with rice — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked chicken (marinated, stir-fried), cooked white rice, and Korean-style dumplings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken bulgogi dosirak as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: (1) marinated stir-fried chicken thigh/breast (USDA FDC #171477 cooked chicken breast, adjusted for bulgogi marinade contribution); (2) cooked short-grain white rice (USDA FDC #169756); (3) pan-fried pork and chive dumplings (estimated from USDA FDC #168927 pork dumplings); (4) typical Korean banchan (seasoned spinach, kimchi). Weighted composite per 100 g of the full 500 g serving. Sodium elevated due to soy-sauce-based marinade and kimchi banchan.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, short-grain) and mixed Korean rice-based meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken bulgogi dosirak as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 based on the dominant glycemic component: cooked short-grain white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The presence of protein (chicken), fat (sesame oil, pork mandu), dietary fibre (banchan vegetables), and vinegar/fermented components (kimchi) is well-documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response by approximately 10–20% relative to plain rice. An analogous mixed Korean rice meal GI of approximately 55–58 is consistent with published data on rice-dominant mixed meals (Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). GI set to 56 (medium category). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (56 × 107.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 60.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-based bulgogi marinade and kimchi banchan
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g dosirak serving provides approximately 2400 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium marinade, limit kimchi portions, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by short-grain white rice, partially attenuated by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (estimated 56) and high glycemic load (GL ~60 per serving) from the white rice component require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion size while increasing the chicken and vegetable banchan proportion can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork mandu and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium load (~2400 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~6.5 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose skinless chicken, and select lower-sodium banchan options to reduce overall atherogenic and hypertensive dietary burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Bulgogi Dosirak (닭불고기 도시락) is a Korean lunchbox meal consisting of thinly sliced chicken marinated in a bulgogi sauce — typically a blend of soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and sometimes gochujang (Korean chilli paste) — that is stir-fried or wok-cooked at high heat to produce caramelised, slightly smoky, tender chicken pieces. The dosirak (도시락) format is a compartmentalised lunchbox that pairs the bulgogi chicken with steamed short-grain white rice, crispy pan-fried pork and chive mandu (dumplings), and two banchan (side dishes), which typically include kimchi and a seasoned vegetable such as spinach namul or bean sprout muchim. The dish is a modern Korean comfort food with roots in traditional bulgogi (불고기, literally 'fire meat') culture, adapted for the convenience lunchbox format popular in Korean restaurants and food courts across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is a balanced mixed meal providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~10 g/100 g) from chicken and mandu, moderate carbohydrate (~21.5 g/100 g) from rice, and moderate fat (~4.8 g/100 g). Sodium is elevated (~480 mg/100 g; ~2400 mg per full serving) due to the soy-sauce marinade and fermented banchan, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component but attenuated by co-ingested protein, fat, and fermented vegetables. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control of the rice component is advisable. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or sesame allergies.

What is the sodium content of chicken bulgogi dosirak?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken bulgogi dosirak as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked marinated chicken, short-grain white rice, pork dumplings, and Korean banchan. The composite weighting is based on a standard 500 g dosirak portion with approximately 40% rice, 35% chicken bulgogi, 10% mandu, and 15% banchan by weight. GI is an analogous estimate from published mixed Korean rice-meal data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported by USDA FoodData Central, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed near-zero value consistent with USDA data for marinated chicken and rice-based dishes with no partially hydrogenated oils. 3. Monounsaturated fat (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 1.8 g from sesame oil and chicken fat components per USDA FDC data). 4. Sodium is notably high (~480 mg/100 g; ~2400 mg per 500 g serving) due to soy-sauce-based bulgogi marinade and kimchi banchan; this is the primary clinical concern for this dish. 5. The dish as served at Ajumma's (Funan, Singapore) may differ in exact marinade formulation, rice quantity, and banchan selection; the nutrient values represent a reasonable composite estimate for a standard chicken bulgogi dosirak of this type. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork mandu; individual restaurants may offer halal-certified versions without pork. 7. GI set to 56 (medium) as an analogous estimate from published data on mixed Korean rice-based meals; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate.
